subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Starshades and Coronagraphs
description: A fast breakdown of Starshades and Coronagraphs for builders.

hook.text: To see an exoplanet next to its star, you must block the star's blinding light. Two technologies compete for this job.

buildup.text: A coronagraph sits inside the telescope and blocks light internally. A starshade flies in space tens of thousands of km ahead.

discovery.text: Coronagraphs have improved a millionfold since the 1930s. Modern ones can suppress starlight by a factor of 10 billion.

twist.text: A starshade must maintain alignment with a distant telescope to within one meter—while both are thousands of km apart.

climax.text: Both approaches are being developed. Whichever succeeds could enable the first true photograph of an Earth-like world.

punchline.text: Block the star, see the planet. Two ideas racing to reveal alien Earths.

quiz.question: What is the main challenge of using a starshade?
options: It must maintain precise alignment with the telescope from thousands of kilometers away
It blocks all light, making it impossible to see the planet too
It can only be used once before needing replacement
